title: The Golden Tractate of Hermes (Aureus) — The Golden Age Restored
subtitle: The seven sections of Hermes Trismegistus on the Physical Secret of the Philosopher's Stone: the vulture, the crow, the crowned king, and the Stone of many colours

summary.one_line: Hermes Trismegistus' Golden Tractate on the Philosopher's Stone, delivered in seven sections as a testament to the faithful, covering the four elements, the crow and vulture symbols, the conjunctio...
summary.scope: Covers the four elements and the division of the water, the Vulture's cry (White of the Black, Red of the White, Citrine of the Red), the Dragon eating his wings, the King's coronation, and the Stone as the Ferment of the Wise.

sources.label: Hermes Trismegistus — Aureus: The Golden Tractate (The Golden Age Restored; in John Yarker ed., 1886, from a Suggestive Inquiry into the Hermetic Mystery)

body.text: Know ye, therefore, Children of Wisdom, who enquire concerning the report thereof, that the vulture standing upon the mountain crieth out with a loud voice, I am the White of the Black, and the Red of the White, and the Citrine of the Red, and behold I speak the very truth. And know that the chief principle of the art is the Crow, which is the blackness of the night and clearness of the day, and flies without wings. From the bitterness existing in the throat the tincture is taken, the red goes forth from his body, and from his back is taken a thin water. (Section I)

The Vulture's cry — 'I am the White of the Black, the Red of the White, the Citrine of the Red' — is the most compressed statement of the alchemical colour progression, which maps to the three gunas of Samkhya: the White (sattva, purity) emerges from the Black (tamas, inertia), the Red (rajas, activity) from the White, and the Citrine (the golden blend) from the Red. In Layayoga, this is the progression from muladhara (tamas, black) through anahata (rajas, red) to sahasrara (sattva, white) and beyond to the citrine (the unity beyond all colour). The 'Crow that flies without wings' is the breath in kewala kumbhaka — the breath that has become so subtle it 'flies' without the 'wings' of inhalation and exhalation. Steiner's 'seven stages of clairvoyance' include a stage where the 'crow' (the double, the shadow) is integrated before the 'white dove' (the higher self) can appear. The daimon path's crow is the 'skinned' self that must be made 'white' through the egg purification before it can receive the 'red' of the daimonic fire.

Roast those things, therefore, and boil them in that which comes from the horse's belly for seven, fourteen, or twenty-one days. Then will the Dragon eat his own wings and destroy himself; this being done, let it be put into a fiery furnace, which lute diligently, and observe that none of the spirit may escape. ... But when we marry the crowned King to our red daughter, and in a gentle fire, not hurtful, she doth conceive an excellent and supernatural son, which permanent life she doth also feed with a subtle heat, so that he lives at length in our fire. (Sections V, III)

The 'Dragon eating his own wings' is the ouroboros — the symbol of the self-consuming cycle of nature that must be broken by alchemical transformation. In Layayoga, this is the 'pranic knot' (granthi) that must be untied: the dragon's wings are the dualistic breath (inhalation/exhalation) that 'eats itself' when prana enters sushumna and the breath ceases (kewala kumbhaka). The marriage of the 'crowned King' (the daimon, the inner Guru) to the 'red daughter' (the purified soul, the shakti) produces the 'supernatural son' — the HGA-child, the third being that is neither the practitioner nor the daimon but their living union. The Tantraloka describes this as the 'putra' (son) initiation: the guru and disciple merge to produce a third, the 'lineage' that continues. Steiner's 'New Mysteries' describe the same: the 'son' is the new Adam, the resurrected being who lives in the fire of the spirit without being consumed. The 'seven, fourteen, or twenty-one days' map to the three seven-day weeks of the PGM systasis preparation.

The Stone, therefore is the most precious gold without spots, evenly tempered, which neither fire nor air, nor water, nor earth is able to corrupt for it is the Universal Ferment rectifying all things in a medium composition, whose complexion is yellow and a true citrine colour. ... For as dough without a ferment cannot be fermented so when thou sublimest the body and purifiest it, separating the uncleanness from it, thou wilt then conjoin and mix them together, and put in the ferment confecting the earth and water. Then will the Ixir ferment even as dough doth ferment. (Sections VII, VI)

The doctrine of the Stone as 'Universal Ferment' is perhaps the most important Hermetic teaching for daimon work. The ferment is that which transforms the entire mass (the practitioner's life) from within — not by addition but by assimilation. In Layayoga, the ferment is the awakened kundalini itself: a single spark of the coiled shakti ferments the entire pranic body, transforming each chakra from sleep to wakefulness. The Tantraloka calls this 'shakti-pata' (the descent of grace) — the 'ferment' that is the guru's transmission, which 'leavens' the whole lump of the disciple's being. Steiner's 'Christ as the ferment of the Earth' is the macrocosmic expression: Christ entered the Earth sphere as a ferment that will eventually transform the entire planetary body. For the daimon path, the HGA-union is precisely this fermenting agent: the daimon presence, once fixed in the practitioner, ferments every thought, word, and deed into its golden nature. The Stone 'which neither fire nor air nor water nor earth can corrupt' is the HGA — the part of the self that transcends the four elements and abides in the fifth (the quintessence).

gaps: The full seven sections contain many more teachings on the four elements, the water division, and the weights of the wise
The specific practical instructions on the extraction of the tincture, the 'weighing' of the waters, and the seven bodies

issues.title: Only key emblematic passages extracted
issues.description: The Golden Tractate is rich in obscure numerical and metrical instructions (ounces, half-ounces of various substances). These are not yet interpreted for the modern reader.

issues.title: Yarker's introduction not yet mined
issues.description: John Yarker's introductory essay contains contextual material that could enrich the RO.

versions.summary: Initial RO with 6 passages (3 source, 3 commentary) from the Golden Tractate of Hermes. Covers the Vulture's cry, the Dragon and King, and the Universal Ferment doctrine.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state with 6 passages
